title: Physical Link Down - Optical Signal Lost
description: Physical/optical link down with complete loss of signal
root_cause_analysis: Complete loss of optical signal between the local SFP and the remote peer. Commonly caused by physical cable disconnect, fibre cut, faulty SFP/transceiver, dirty connectors, or remote peer shutdown.

symptoms: oper_status is down
admin_status is up
LOS (Loss of Signal) alarm raised
No optical Rx power detected
negative_indicators: admin_status is down — see link.admin_down
Interface is flapping — see link.flap
CRC/input errors present while link up — see link.high_errors
admin_status is down — this is an intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
flap_count > 3 and oper_changes_per_min > 1 — interface is bouncing, see link.flap
flap_count elevated AND crc_errors rising — combined failure, see link.flap_with_errors
lag_active_members reduced but LAG still up — bundle member lost, see link.lag_degraded
lag_oper_status is down — aggregate issue, see link.lag_down
optical_rx_power_dbm trending down but link still up — see link.degraded
log_patterns: %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface .*, changed state to down

hypotheses.description: Physical cable disconnected or fibre pulled
Fibre cut or break in the physical plant
SFP/XFP optical module failure
Dirty or contaminated optical connector
Remote peer interface shutdown

render_template: Link/Interface {interface} on device {device} is down. Operational status is {oper_status}, while administrative status is {admin_status}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Interface Administratively Shutdown
description: Interface is intentionally shut down by operator or automation
root_cause_analysis: The interface has admin_status=down, indicating it was explicitly disabled via CLI, automation, or policy. This is not a fault unless unintended.

symptoms: oper_status is down
admin_status is down
Recent configuration change
negative_indicators: LOS alarm present — see link.down
Interface flapping
LOS alarm present on interface — physical fault present, see link.down
flap_count elevated — admin_status may be cycling, check link.flap
crc_errors or in_errors rising — data-plane fault, see link.high_errors
No config change in audit log — likely physical failure, see link.down
lag_oper_status is down — LAG-level issue, see link.lag_down
All interfaces on same slot down — hardware issue, see device.linecard_failure
Multiple interfaces simultaneously admin-down — check automation or policy, see device.unreachable
log_patterns: %LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface .*, changed state to administratively down
%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface .*, changed state to down
%PM-4-ERR_DISABLE: .* error detected on .*, putting .* in err-disable state
%STORM_CONTROL-3-SHUTDOWN: A port was shutdown due to storm-control
interface .* placed in shutdown state by automation
Interface .* admin state changed to down
snmp set: ifAdminStatus = down on interface .*

hypotheses.description: Manual operator shutdown
Automation script issued shutdown
Security/policy enforcement disabled port
Port in err-disabled state

render_template: Interface {interface} on device {device} is administratively down.

title: Unidirectional / One-Way Link Failure
description: Link appears up locally but only one direction is passing traffic — classic split-brain or unidirectional failure
root_cause_analysis: A one-way (unidirectional) link exists when the local interface shows oper_status=up but the remote peer cannot receive frames (or vice versa). This is particularly dangerous as routing protocols may not detect the failure immediately, leading to black-holing of traffic. Caused by a failed Tx or Rx path in one direction (e.g. one fibre strand broken, one SFP laser dead, Rx only failed), or UDLD/BFD timeout.

symptoms: Local oper_status is up, remote oper_status is also up
Traffic flows in one direction only
UDLD neighbour messages not received from peer
BFD session down while interface remains up
Routing protocol adjacency loss (OSPF/BGP) without physical link alarm
High in_discards or zero counters on one side
negative_indicators: Full bidirectional traffic confirmed by ICMP echo-reply
oper_status is down — see link.down instead
Interface is flapping — see link.flap
ICMP echo-reply succeeds bidirectionally — link is fully operational, not one-way
oper_status is down with LOS — complete physical failure, see link.down
interface is flapping — see link.flap or link.flap_with_errors
Both BFD and UDLD are UP — link is bidirectional, check application layer
lag_active_members reduced — member-level issue, see link.lag_degraded
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
crc_errors rising on up link — data corruption not directionality, see link.high_errors
log_patterns: %BFD-5-SESSION_DOWN: BFD session for neighbor .* is down
OSPF: .* neighbor .* down - Dead timer expired
BGP: .* neighbor .* Down - Hold timer expired
bfd: session .* moved to down state
rx power normal, tx power absent
UDLD: port .* put in errdisable state

hypotheses.description: One strand of a duplex fibre pair is broken — Tx or Rx path severed
One direction of the SFP (Tx laser or Rx photodiode) has failed
UDLD protocol detected and err-disabled the unidirectional port
Tx/Rx fibres swapped or miscrossed at patch panel

keywords: routing adjacency dropped on up interface
render_template: Interface {interface} on device {device} has a one-way link condition. Local status is {oper_status} but traffic is unidirectional. BFD state: {bfd_session_state}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Interface Flapping with Concurrent High Error Rate
description: Interface oscillates between up/down states AND exhibits elevated CRC/input/output errors — combined physical and logical failure
root_cause_analysis: Unlike a simple link flap (negotiation or loose cable), flap-with-errors indicates physical-layer degradation that is simultaneously causing signal instability and data corruption. The interface may briefly come up during each cycle but corrupts frames before dropping again. Typical causes: severely degraded optical signal near sensitivity threshold, failing SFP with intermittent Tx, or a partially damaged fibre.

symptoms: flap_count is elevated (>3 in observation window)
oper_changes_per_min is elevated
crc_errors or in_errors are rising even during up intervals
Optical Rx power is near or below minimum receive sensitivity
Protocol adjacencies (BGP, OSPF, LDP) cycling with the interface
negative_indicators: Interface is stable (no flaps) — see link.high_errors instead
Interface is permanently down — see link.down
No errors during up periods — see link.flap
Interface is stable (no flaps) and only errors present — see link.high_errors
Interface is permanently down with LOS — see link.down
No errors during up intervals — see link.flap for clean flap
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
lag_active_members reduced — bundle member lost, see link.lag_degraded
BFD/UDLD down but interface is up — unidirectional failure, see link.one_way
optical_rx_power_dbm trending but no flap yet — pre-failure, see link.degraded
log_patterns: %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol .*, changed state to down
crc errors increasing on flapping interface
optical rx power: .* dBm - below sensitivity
physical layer: signal quality below threshold

hypotheses.description: Optical signal near or below receiver sensitivity — marginal connection causing flap+errors
SFP in early-failure mode — intermittent laser output causing combined flap and error symptoms
Micro-bend, kink, or partial fibre damage that worsens under temperature or vibration
Speed/duplex mismatch causing late collisions and CRC errors alongside flapping

render_template: Interface {resource_name} on {device} is flapping ({flap_count} flaps, {oper_changes_per_min}/min) with concurrent errors (in_errors={in_errors}, crc_errors={crc_errors}). Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Link Degraded - Rising BER / Marginal Optical Power
description: Interface remains operationally up but optical quality is deteriorating — a precursor to full link failure
root_cause_analysis: The link is still carrying traffic but optical Rx power is trending toward the minimum sensitivity threshold, or BER (Bit Error Rate) is rising above acceptable levels. This is a pre-failure condition that, if unaddressed, will progress to link.down or link.flap_with_errors. Common causes: slow fibre degradation, connector oxidation, SFP aging, or temperature-induced optical drift.

symptoms: oper_status is up
optical_rx_power_dbm trending down toward threshold
BER rising above 1e-9
FEC (Forward Error Correction) correction rate increasing
Intermittent low-level CRC errors appearing
DOM temperature or voltage moving outside normal range
negative_indicators: oper_status is down — see link.down
Interface is flapping — see link.flap or link.flap_with_errors
Optical power is stable and within spec
oper_status is down — link has already failed, see link.down
flap_count elevated — link is already unstable, see link.flap or link.flap_with_errors
crc_errors high but optical power stable — data-plane issue, see link.high_errors
Optical power is stable and within spec — no degradation present
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
lag_active_members reduced — bundle member fault, see link.lag_degraded
BFD session down on up link — unidirectionality, see link.one_way
log_patterns: DOM: Rx power .* dBm - approaching sensitivity limit
DOM: Tx power .* dBm - below normal range
FEC: correcting .* errors per second on interface
Q-factor below threshold on optical channel
OSNR: .* dB below minimum for modulation
FEC correction rate: .* percent - threshold exceeded
DOM: voltage .* V outside normal range
optical path: insertion loss .* dB exceeds budget
pre-los: rx power trending toward sensitivity limit

hypotheses.description: Gradual oxidation or contamination of optical connectors reducing throughput
SFP/XFP nearing end-of-life — Tx laser or Rx photodiode output declining
Accumulated micro-bends, splitter losses, or DWDM amplifier gain compression
Environmental temperature change causing optical wavelength drift or DOM alarm

render_template: Interface {interface} on device {device} is degraded. Optical Rx power: {optical_rx_power_dbm} dBm, BER: {ber}. Proactive attention required before link failure. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Interface Flapping - Repeated Up/Down State Changes
description: Interface flapping frequently without significant concurrent error rate
root_cause_analysis: Interface repeatedly toggling between up and down states. Typically caused by loose cable, speed/duplex negotiation mismatch, or marginal signal quality. Distinguish from link.flap_with_errors where CRC/input errors are also elevated.

symptoms: interface changed state repeatedly
flap_count is elevated (>3 in window)
oper_changes_per_min is elevated (>1/min)
No persistent LOS alarm
Protocol adjacencies cycling
negative_indicators: oper_status is permanently down — see link.down
LOS or optical alarm present
CRC or input errors elevated — see link.flap_with_errors
oper_status permanently down with LOS — see link.down
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
crc_errors or in_errors elevated during up periods — see link.flap_with_errors
optical_rx_power_dbm below threshold — optical degradation, see link.degraded
lag_active_members reduced — bundle member lost, see link.lag_degraded
BFD/UDLD down on stable-up interface — unidirectional, see link.one_way
Multiple devices affected simultaneously with broadcast storm — see link.stp_loop
log_patterns: %LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface .*, changed state to down
ifOperStatus: .* down, then up within .* seconds
duplex mismatch: local full, remote half
LACP: member .* removed from bundle due to timeout
stp: port .* changed state to forwarding then blocking
physical: intermittent signal loss without LOS alarm
negotiation: auto speed failed — defaulting to 10M

hypotheses.description: Loose connector or intermittently failing cable causing brief LOS cycles
Speed/Duplex auto-negotiation mismatch triggering link resets
Keepalive timer mismatch causing protocol-driven link bouncing
Spanning Tree topology change causing repeated port state transitions

render_template: Interface {resource_name} on {device} is flapping ({flap_count} flaps, {oper_changes_per_min} state changes/min). No significant concurrent errors. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Elevated Interface Error Rate - CRC / Input / Output Errors
description: High CRC/Input/Output errors on an otherwise stable, operationally-up interface
root_cause_analysis: High rate of CRC, input, or output errors on an otherwise-up interface. Usually indicates physical-layer corruption (bad cable, faulty optics) or control-plane congestion (buffer overflow, output queue drops). The interface is not flapping — pure data-plane degradation.

symptoms: crc_errors or in_errors rising consistently
oper_status remains up
out_discards or output drops increasing
Application throughput affected despite link being up
SNMP MIB counters for ifInErrors, ifOutDiscards non-zero and growing
negative_indicators: oper_status is down with LOS — see link.down
Interface is flapping — see link.flap_with_errors
Hard link-down symptoms dominate
oper_status is down with LOS — complete failure, see link.down
admin_status is down — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
flap_count elevated alongside errors — combined failure, see link.flap_with_errors
Interface is flapping — errors may be artefact of flap, see link.flap
optical_rx_power_dbm trending down — pre-failure, see link.degraded
lag_active_members reduced — member lost from bundle, see link.lag_degraded
cpu_utilization_percent high — device-level issue causing drops, see device.cpu_overload
BFD/UDLD down but link up — check link.one_way before treating as error-only
log_patterns: %COUNTERS-3-INPUT_ERROR_RATE: Input error rate exceeded threshold on interface
%COUNTERS-3-OUTPUT_DROP_RATE: Output drop rate exceeded threshold on interface
CRC error count: .* on interface .* - threshold exceeded
output drops: .* packets dropped on interface
queue full: tail-drop active on interface
FCS errors: .* on interface .* - threshold crossed
ifInErrors counter: rising faster than traffic rate
output queue depth .* exceeded - drops active

hypotheses.description: CRC errors from physical-layer corruption — bad cable, connector, or optics
Output queue overflow causing tail-drop discards on a congested interface
Half/Full duplex mismatch producing late collisions that register as input errors
MTU mismatch causing oversized frames to be dropped as errors or giants

render_template: Interface {resource_name} on {device} shows elevated errors (in_errors={in_errors}, crc_errors={crc_errors}, out_discards={out_discards}). Link is up. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device Not Reachable - Complete Management Loss
description: Device is unreachable from the management system — all polling methods (SNMP, ICMP, gRPC) have failed
root_cause_analysis: Device is not responding to any management queries. This is a broad catch-all that must be quickly triaged into sub-causes: power failure, control-plane crash, upstream routing loss to management IP, or configuration change locking out management access. Use correlated events (other devices in the same PoP, same PSU circuit, same management VLAN) to narrow the hypothesis.

symptoms: Device Not Reachable alarm from NMS
SNMP polling failed — timeout or no response
ICMP ping to management IP fails
gRPC/NETCONF session dropped
All interfaces on device showing stale data
No syslog received from device for >N minutes
negative_indicators: Only one interface down but device responds on other IPs — see link.down
Only SSH failing while SNMP responds — may be mgmt-plane partial failure
Only one application failing — application-layer issue, not device.unreachable
Only one interface down but other IPs respond — physical failure, see link.down
Only SSH fails while SNMP responds — partial mgmt issue, not full device.unreachable
psu_status is failed — power as root cause, escalate to device.power_failure
cpu_utilization_percent > 90 — management blackout from CPU, see device.cpu_overload
sysUpTime very low and device recovered — it rebooted, see device.unexpected_reboot
sysUpTime cycling repeatedly — boot loop, see device.boot_loop
ha_state shows switchover — HA event, see device.supervisor_failover
linecard_status failed — partial outage only, see device.linecard_failure
log_patterns: SNMP: Timeout on .* - no response from device
ICMP: ping to .* failed - host unreachable
NMS: device .* marked as down - polling failure
syslog: no messages received from .* for .* minutes
SNMP: all retries exhausted for host .*
management plane: SSH connection to .* refused
SSH: connection to .* timed out after .* seconds

hypotheses.description: Power supply failure or device fully powered off
Control-plane or management process crash / CPUHOG preventing response
Loss of routing or firewall policy to management IP — device is up but unreachable from NMS
Configuration change removed management access (e.g. ACL, VRF move, SNMP community change)

render_template: Device {device} is not reachable (availability={availability}%). All management protocols failing. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device Power Failure - PSU or Feed Loss
description: One or more power supply units have failed, or the primary power feed has been lost
root_cause_analysis: Power events are among the highest-impact failures as they affect all services on the device simultaneously. A single-PSU failure on a dual-PSU device may not cause an outage but leaves no redundancy. A dual-PSU loss or main feed failure causes total device outage. Correlated with UPS events, circuit breaker trips, or datacenter power alarms. Physical site visit is often required to confirm.

symptoms: PSU alarm or power module failure trap received
Device unreachable immediately after power event
UPS or PDU event correlated in same rack/circuit
Multiple devices in same PoP going unreachable simultaneously
Environmental sensor (temp/fan) alarms if device is partially up
Last syslog message may reference 'system going down' or 'power loss'
negative_indicators: Device responds via OOB — power may be partial or PSU redundancy held
Only one PSU alarmed and device is still reachable — degraded redundancy, not outage
Software crash evident in logs before power event
Device responds via OOB — single PSU failed but device held, not full outage
Only one PSU alarmed and device reachable — degraded redundancy, not device.power_failure outage
Software crash evident in logs before power — software cause, see device.unexpected_reboot
cpu_utilization_percent > 90 before failure — CPU cause, see device.cpu_overload
sysUpTime cycling — boot loop consuming power, see device.boot_loop
ha_state shows switchover — HA event not power, see device.supervisor_failover
linecard_status failed only — chassis power OK, see device.linecard_failure
Link interfaces down but device management responds — not power, check link.down
log_patterns: UPS: transfer to battery power initiated
circuit breaker: trip detected on panel .*
chassis: power budget exceeded - shedding load
PSU .* input voltage out of range

hypotheses.description: Both PSUs or the shared power feed have lost power — full device outage
One PSU has failed on a device without full N+1 redundancy — device may still operate
Datacenter or facility-level power event affecting an entire circuit or row
Device initiated protective thermal shutdown due to overheating (fan failure or cooling loss)

render_template: Device {device} has a power failure event. PSU status: {psu_status}. Availability: {availability}%. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device CPU Overload - Control Plane Saturated
description: Device CPU is critically high, starving management and control-plane processes of cycles
root_cause_analysis: Control-plane CPU saturation causes management processes (SNMP agent, SSH, syslog) to become unresponsive, while data-plane forwarding (hardware-based) may continue. This creates a misleading state where traffic appears to flow but the device cannot be managed or queried. Causes include: routing protocol instability flooding the CPU with LSAs/updates, a traffic-punt attack, runaway software process, or memory pressure causing paging.

symptoms: cpu_utilization_percent persistently >90%
SNMP response timeouts — NMS marks device unreachable or degraded
SSH/Telnet connection refused or extremely slow
BGP or OSPF adjacencies dropping due to hello timeout
CPUHOG or high CPU syslog messages
Management plane process showing very high CPU in 'show processes'
negative_indicators: CPU is normal — management failure may be network-path issue, see device.unreachable
Device is fully unreachable — may be power failure, see device.power_failure
Memory is normal and no runaway process visible
CPU is normal — management failure is network-path issue, see device.unreachable
Device is fully unreachable with no CPU data — see device.power_failure or device.unexpected_reboot
memory_utilization_percent > 90 — memory root cause, see device.memory_exhaustion
psu_status is failed — power issue driving reset, see device.power_failure
sysUpTime very low — device just rebooted, see device.unexpected_reboot
linecard_status failed — FPC issue not CPU, see device.linecard_failure
ha_state changed — supervisor event, see device.supervisor_failover
Broadcast storm on L2 — external cause of CPU spike, check link.stp_loop first
log_patterns: %SYS-3-CPUHOG: Task .* has been running for .* ms
%BGP-3-NOTIFICATION: received from neighbor .* - hold time expired
%OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process .* Nbr .* from FULL to DOWN, Neighbor Down
CPU: .* percent utilization, .* percent in interrupt context
SNMP: agent response time .* ms - above threshold
control-plane: punt rate .* pps - threshold exceeded
CoPP: class .* dropping .* pps - rate exceeded
BGP: memory allocation failure - hold timer expired
OSPF: dead timer expired - high cpu prevented hello
process .* schedule latency .* ms - CPU contention
BGP update storm: .* prefixes received in .* seconds

hypotheses.description: Route flap storm or BGP/OSPF update flood overloading the routing process
High rate of packets being punted from hardware to software plane (DDoS or scanning traffic)
Software bug or misconfiguration causing a process to consume all CPU cycles
Memory exhaustion causing kernel paging that drives CPU high

render_template: Device {device} CPU is at {cpu_utilization_percent}% — management plane unresponsive. SNMP response time: {snmp_response_time_ms}ms. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device Unexpected Reboot / Crash-Reload
description: Device reloaded unexpectedly — no planned maintenance window associated with the event
root_cause_analysis: An unplanned reboot causes a brief total outage during reload (typically 3–15 minutes for most platforms). The device may come back fully functional, or it may reload into a degraded state (boot loop, partial supervisor, rommon). Root cause must be established from the reload reason and crash info before the next occurrence. Common causes: software panic/kernel crash, hardware fault (memory ECC error, supervisor crash), watchdog expiry, or in rare cases an accidental 'reload' command.

symptoms: Device was unreachable for a period and returned
Uptime counter reset — sysUpTime is low (e.g. <30 minutes)
Reload reason captured in 'show version' or syslog
Crash file or core dump present on bootflash
All protocol adjacencies reset and reconverging
NMS shows availability dip then recovery
negative_indicators: Device is still unreachable — reboot may not have completed, see device.unreachable
Uptime is normal — no actual reload occurred
Planned maintenance window in change management system
Device is still unreachable — reboot may not have completed, see device.unreachable
Uptime is normal — no reload occurred, investigate other alarms
Planned maintenance window confirmed in change management — not unexpected
sysUpTime cycling every few minutes — boot loop, see device.boot_loop
ha_state shows switchover before uptime reset — HA event, see device.supervisor_failover
psu_status failed at time of reload — power cause, see device.power_failure
linecard_status failed but device is still up — not a full reboot, see device.linecard_failure
log_patterns: %SYS-5-RELOAD: Reload requested by .* on vty.* Reload Reason: .*
%SYS-6-BOOTTIME: Time taken to reboot after reload = .* seconds
SNMP: sysUpTime reset to 0 on device .*
system: unexpected reload - core file written to bootflash
watchdog: .* did not check in within .* seconds - reloading
ECC: uncorrectable memory error - initiating reload
bus error: .* at PC .* - system reloading

hypotheses.description: OS software panic, kernel crash, or process watchdog expiry caused an auto-reload
Hardware-induced crash — ECC memory error, supervisor fault, or bus error
System watchdog expired because CPU was too busy to service it (often follows CPU overload)
Operator accidentally issued a reload command or automation triggered an unplanned reload

render_template: Device {device} has unexpectedly rebooted. Current uptime: {sys_uptime_seconds}s. Reload reason: {reload_reason}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: LAG / Port-Channel Completely Down - All Members Lost
description: Link Aggregation Group (LAG/LACP/port-channel/bundle) is fully down — no active member interfaces remain
root_cause_analysis: A LAG going completely down means every member link has failed OR the LACP control protocol has determined the bundle is unusable (min-links threshold breached, actor/partner system-ID mismatch, or PDU timeout). This is a service-affecting event because the logical interface carries aggregated traffic from all members. Critical distinction from link.down: the physical member ports may still show oper_status=up while the LAG itself is down — e.g., all members up but LACP negotiation failed. Always inspect the LAG-level state first, then drill into member-level state and LACP PDU exchange logs.

symptoms: LAG/port-channel interface oper_status is down
lag_active_members equals zero
All member interfaces may be individually up but LACP is not converged
LACP PDU timeout or mismatch syslog on one or both sides
Downstream routing protocols (BGP/OSPF) drop adjacency over the LAG
Traffic counters on LAG go to zero — no Tx or Rx
min-links threshold alarm if configured
negative_indicators: At least one LAG member is active — LAG is not fully down, see link.lag_degraded
Physical member oper_status is down with LOS — physical issue, check link.down on member
Single physical interface down with no LAG context — see link.down
lag_active_members > 0 — LAG is partially up, see link.lag_degraded
Only one member down with LAG still up — see link.lag_degraded
Physical member LOS without LACP involvement — check link.down on member
admin_status is down on LAG — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
Device fully unreachable not just LAG — see device.unreachable
All ports on single slot failed — hardware fault, see device.linecard_failure
STP topology change caused LAG state — check link.stp_loop
Remote device rebooted — consider device.unexpected_reboot on peer
log_patterns: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface Port-channel.*, changed state to down
%LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface Bundle-Ether.*, changed state to down
LACP: bundle .* is down - no active members
LACP: system-ID mismatch on .* - bundle cannot form
LACP: port-key mismatch on member .* - expelled from bundle
ae.*: all member links are down - aggregate down
port-channel.*: min-links .* not satisfied - going down
LACP: partner timeout on all members of bundle .*
aggregation .* has no active members - operationally down
LACP: fast PDU timeout on interface .* - removing from bundle

hypotheses.description: All physical member links have gone down simultaneously (fibre cut, remote end rebooted, or upstream device failure)
LACP PDUs stopped being received from the peer — peer restarted, timer misconfiguration, or control-plane overload suppressed LACP on remote end
LACP actor/partner system-ID or key mismatch — member ports assigned to wrong bundle, misconfigured channel-group number, or duplicate system priority
min-links policy configured — enough members failed to drop below the minimum active-member threshold, forcing the bundle down
Remote peer device rebooted or its port-channel was removed, causing all LACP PDUs to stop from that side

render_template: LAG interface {resource_name} on device {device} is fully down. Active members: {lag_active_members}/{lag_total_members}. LACP state: {lacp_state}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: LAG Degraded - One or More Members Lost, Reduced Bandwidth
description: LAG/port-channel is operationally up but has fewer active members than configured — bandwidth is reduced and redundancy is compromised
root_cause_analysis: A degraded LAG is among the most dangerous failure modes in enterprise and carrier networks because it remains functionally operational — traffic continues to flow — while silently operating below intended capacity. Monitoring systems often show the LAG as 'green' while its bandwidth has halved or worse. This condition can persist undetected until the remaining members also fail, causing a sudden full outage. The root cause is typically a single physical member failure, an LACP PDU loss on one member, or a misconfiguration applied to a subset of members. Must be distinguished from link.lag_down (all members lost) and link.down (individual physical interface failure not part of a LAG).

symptoms: LAG oper_status is up but lag_active_members < lag_configured_members
lag_member_down_count is greater than zero
Throughput on the LAG is throttled — ECMP load-balancing is uneven
LACP partner reports fewer active ports than expected
One or more member interfaces show oper_status=down or are in LACP suspended state
Bandwidth utilization may spike on remaining members as traffic re-distributes
No min-links alarm if remaining members meet the threshold
negative_indicators: lag_active_members equals zero — LAG is fully down, see link.lag_down instead
All configured members are active — no degradation, false alert
Traffic loss is total — may have crossed min-links, see link.lag_down
lag_active_members equals zero — LAG fully down, see link.lag_down
All configured members active — no degradation, false alert
Traffic loss is total — crossed min-links threshold, see link.lag_down
admin_status is down on LAG — intentional shutdown, see link.admin_down
Physical LOS on member — drill into member with link.down
Device is fully unreachable — escalate to device.unreachable
STP port transitions on member — check link.stp_loop or link.flap
log_patterns: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface .* (member of Port-channel.*), changed state to down
LACP: member .* suspended from bundle .* - PDU timeout
LACP: port .* defaulted - no PDUs received from partner
ae.*: member interface .* removed - .* members remaining
Bundle-Ether.*: member .* down, .* of .* members active
port-channel.*: member .* removed, bandwidth reduced to .*
LACP: member .* expelled - speed mismatch with bundle
lag member .* MTU mismatch - removed from bundle
lag load-balancing: uneven distribution detected after member loss
port-channel member count: .* of .* expected members active
ae.*: bandwidth reduced to .* Gbps after member loss
LACP: member .* errdisabled on remote peer - timeout

hypotheses.description: One or more physical member interfaces have failed (SFP, cable, or remote port) reducing active member count
Member entered LACP suspended/defaulted state due to PDU timeout or negotiation failure on that member only
One member auto-negotiated to a lower speed than other members, causing LACP to reject or suspend it from the bundle
A configuration change (VLAN, QoS, MTU) applied inconsistently across members caused LACP to expel the non-conforming member
The remote peer port for one member was administratively disabled or errdisabled, causing that member's LACP to time out

render_template: LAG interface {resource_name} on device {device} is degraded: {lag_active_members} of {lag_configured_members} members active. Available bandwidth reduced. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Spanning Tree Loop - Layer 2 Broadcast Storm
description: A Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) loop has formed, causing a broadcast storm that is consuming all available bandwidth and CPU on affected switches
root_cause_analysis: An STP loop occurs when a port that should be in STP blocking state erroneously transitions to forwarding, creating a cycle in the L2 topology. Broadcast, multicast, and unknown-unicast frames loop indefinitely, doubling every round-trip until bandwidth is exhausted and switch CPUs spike to 100%. Unlike almost all other link failures, the physical interfaces remain operationally up — the loop is a control-plane and forwarding-plane failure. This is one of the most severe and rapidly escalating L2 faults: a full broadcast storm can saturate a 10G link within seconds. Detection relies on correlated spikes across multiple devices simultaneously, not a single interface going down. Root causes include: BPDU Guard not configured on access ports, an unmanaged switch inserted by a user, STP root election upset, or a PortFast misconfiguration on a trunk port.

symptoms: CPU utilization spikes to >90% on multiple switches in the same L2 domain simultaneously
Broadcast/multicast counters rising exponentially on multiple interfaces
Switch MAC address table continuously flushing and re-learning — high mac_flap_count
All or most end-hosts in the affected VLAN lose connectivity simultaneously
SNMP polling on affected switches becomes slow or times out (CPU consumed)
Syslog may show 'topology change' notifications or MAC flap warnings
Storm-control or rate-limiter triggers on affected ports
Physical interfaces remain up — no LOS alarm
negative_indicators: Only one switch affected — unlikely to be an STP loop, check device.cpu_overload
Physical interfaces are down — not an STP loop scenario
CPU spike has a single process as cause — check device.cpu_overload (routing storm, punt attack)
Affected hosts are in different VLANs — check for routing or inter-VLAN issue instead
Only one switch affected — CPU issue not L2 loop, see device.cpu_overload
Physical interfaces are down — not an STP loop, check link.down
Hosts in different VLANs affected — routing/inter-VLAN issue, not stp_loop
Single process consuming CPU — not storm-driven, see device.cpu_overload
Broadcast rate normal but CPU high — routing storm, see device.cpu_overload
BFD/UDLD session down — unidirectional link possible cause, see link.one_way
LAG members cycling — bundle instability, see link.lag_degraded or link.lag_down
log_patterns: %SPANTREE-2-LOOPGUARD_BLOCK: Loop guard blocking port .* on VLAN .*
%MAC_MOVE-4-NOTIF: Host .* in vlan .* is flapping between port .* and port .*
spanning-tree: topology change detected on port .*
mac address-table: .* entries flushed due to topology change
broadcast storm: .* Mbps on interface .* - storm-control active
STP: superior BPDU received - new root bridge .*
RSTP: port .* moved to forwarding without delay - possible loop
interface .* input broadcast rate: .* pps - above threshold

hypotheses.description: An unmanaged (dumb) switch inserted by a user with two ports connected back to the managed network — no STP to break the loop
PortFast-enabled access port received a BPDU from a downstream switch — if BPDU Guard is missing, the port stays forwarding and the downstream switch forms a loop
A new switch with a lower bridge priority or MAC address was connected and preempted the STP root, triggering a full topology reconvergence that created a temporary loop
PortFast was misconfigured on a trunk or uplink port, bypassing STP and causing a loop when another switch was connected downstream
A unidirectional failure on an uplink caused an alternate port to transition from blocking to forwarding (Loop Guard not configured), creating a loop

render_template: STP loop detected in L2 domain containing device {device}, VLAN {vlan}. CPU: {cpu_utilization_percent}%, broadcast rate: {broadcast_rate_pps} pps, MAC flaps: {mac_flap_count}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Linecard / FPC Hardware Failure - Partial Device Outage
description: A linecard, FPC (Flexible PIC Concentrator), or line module has faulted or been taken offline — all interfaces on that card are lost while the device chassis and supervisor remain reachable
root_cause_analysis: Linecard failures are a distinct class from full device outages: the supervisor, management plane, and all ports on healthy cards remain fully operational. Only the ports physically resident on the failed card go dark. This makes the fault appear as a simultaneous multi-interface link-down event correlated to a single slot, rather than a device-level outage. Causes range from ASIC hardware faults and ECC memory errors in the forwarding engine to power budget exhaustion in the chassis (not enough wattage to power the card), overheating, or a firmware panic on the FPC. On Juniper MX/PTX platforms this surfaces as FPC offline/restart events; on Cisco ASR/Catalyst as linecard 'faulty' or LC offline; on Nokia SR as MDA/MDA failure. Critical distinction from device.unreachable: management is alive, only a slot's worth of interfaces are affected.

symptoms: All interfaces in slot N simultaneously go to oper_status=down
Chassis/platform diagnostics show slot N as 'faulty', 'offline', or 'failed'
Device itself remains reachable via management IP — SNMP polling succeeds
FPC/linecard restart or offline syslog on the device
Hardware alarm (major or critical) raised for the affected slot
OBFL (Onboard Failure Logging) or chassis log contains hardware error for slot N
Affected slot may show repeated restart attempts (FPC going online then offline)
negative_indicators: All interfaces on the device are down and device is unreachable — see device.unreachable
Only one interface is down, not all interfaces on the same slot — see link.down
Supervisor is the faulted component — see device.supervisor_failover
Device is in a boot loop — see device.boot_loop
All interfaces on device down AND device unreachable — full outage, see device.unreachable
Only one interface down from that slot — single port failure, see link.down
Supervisor is the failed component — see device.supervisor_failover
Device in boot loop — not a linecard issue, see device.boot_loop
psu_status failed — power cause, see device.power_failure
ha_state shows switchover — HA event, see device.supervisor_failover
log_patterns: FPC .* going offline - reason: hardware error
slot .* powered off by power management
linecard .* ASIC .* uncorrectable error - taking offline
OBFL: hardware error logged for slot .* at .*
PFE: packet forwarding engine error on FPC .* - offline
NPA/NPU error: slot .* forwarding engine failure

hypotheses.description: Forwarding ASIC or NPU hardware fault — uncorrectable error in the packet processing engine, triggering FPC offline by the OS
Uncorrectable ECC memory error in the FPC's DRAM or SRAM — OS detects the error and takes the FPC offline to prevent silent data corruption
Chassis power budget insufficient to support all installed linecards — the lowest-priority card is shut down by the power management system
FPC overheated due to inadequate airflow or fan failure — thermal protection shut the card offline to prevent permanent damage
FPC microkernel or RTOS crash due to a software bug — the FPC reboots automatically but may enter a crash loop if the bug is triggered consistently

render_template: Linecard/FPC in slot {slot_id} on device {device} has failed. Status: {linecard_status}. {slot_interface_down_count} interfaces affected. Device management is reachable. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Supervisor / Route Engine Failover - HA Switchover Event
description: The active supervisor, Route Engine (RE), or RSP has failed over to the standby — control-plane traffic interrupted briefly; data-plane may be preserved via NSF/NSR/GRES
root_cause_analysis: Modern carrier-grade and enterprise core devices run dual supervisors/Route Engines for high availability. When the active supervisor fails or is administratively switched over, the standby takes control. This produces a signature that NMS interprets as 'device down then up' — but it is fundamentally different from a true outage. With NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding) or GRES (Graceful Routing Engine Switchover), the data-plane forwarding table survives and traffic continues; routing protocol adjacencies may briefly blip. Without NSF/GRES, all adjacencies reset and convergence time applies. Critical to distinguish from device.unexpected_reboot (single supervisor crash/reload) and device.unreachable (no recovery). Always check whether the event was planned (maintenance window) before escalating, and determine whether data-plane traffic was preserved.

symptoms: Active supervisor changes — syslog indicates standby took over
Device management briefly unreachable then returns — uptime continues from before switchover (NSF) or resets
Syslog shows 'switchover' or 'standby becoming active'
HA state transitions from active/standby to active/active briefly then restabilizes
Routing protocol adjacencies may blip (if non-NSF) or hold (if NSF/GRES active)
SNMP sysUpTime may NOT reset if NSF preserved forwarding state
Hardware alarm cleared after standby becomes stable active
negative_indicators: Device is still unreachable — standby may also be down, see device.unreachable
sysUpTime fully reset and device took many minutes to recover — likely device.unexpected_reboot
Only one supervisor installed — failover is impossible, this is device.unexpected_reboot
Linecard interfaces are lost but device is reachable — see device.linecard_failure
Device is still unreachable after brief outage — standby also failed, see device.unreachable
sysUpTime fully reset and long recovery — full reload, see device.unexpected_reboot
Only one supervisor installed — no HA possible, see device.unexpected_reboot
Linecard interfaces lost but device reachable — see device.linecard_failure
psu_status failed — power cause of switchover, check device.power_failure
memory_utilization_percent critically high — OOM crash on active RE, see device.memory_exhaustion
log_patterns: HA: keepalive failure detected - initiating switchover

hypotheses.description: Active supervisor experienced a software or hardware fault — OS watchdog triggered a failover to the standby
Hardware fault (ECC error, power issue, or thermal) on the active supervisor triggered an automatic HA switchover
HA keepalive between active and standby supervisors timed out — standby assumed primary was dead and preempted, possibly due to a link failure between the two supervisors or CPU overload on active
Operator or automation triggered a manual supervisor switchover for maintenance or upgrade
In-Service Software Upgrade (ISSU) triggered a controlled HA switchover as part of the upgrade process

render_template: Supervisor/RE failover detected on device {device}. Active supervisor changed from slot {old_supervisor_slot} to {active_supervisor_slot}. HA state: {ha_state}. NSF active: {nsf_active}. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device Boot Loop - Stuck in Repeated Restart Cycle
description: Device is caught in a continuous boot loop — it becomes briefly reachable during boot then crashes and restarts, never reaching a stable operating state
root_cause_analysis: A boot loop is one of the most severe device failure modes because the device is stuck in a state where it cannot self-recover. It appears to NMS as a series of rapid device.unreachable / available cycles, with uptime never exceeding a few minutes. The device may be stuck in rommon (ROM Monitor mode), repeatedly crashing during OS initialization, or failing POST (Power-On Self Test). Unlike device.unexpected_reboot (a one-time crash that recovers normally), a boot loop is a persistent, cyclic condition that requires manual console access to resolve. Causes include: a corrupt or incompatible boot image following a failed upgrade, persistent OS software bugs triggered at startup, hardware DIMM failures that prevent memory initialization, or a broken file system on the boot media. Time-to-resolution is high — always requires hands-on or console access, and often a field dispatch.

symptoms: Device availability oscillates — briefly reachable then unreachable in short cycles (<5 minutes per cycle)
sysUpTime resets every cycle — always very low
SNMP polling succeeds intermittently only during the brief up window
Console shows repeated boot sequence starting over — rommon prompt or crash-restart
Syslog shows the same initialization messages repeating
POST failure messages appear during the up window
Device never fully converges — no routing adjacencies establish
negative_indicators: Device is permanently unreachable without cycling — may be device.power_failure or device.unreachable
Device stabilizes after one or two reboots — likely device.unexpected_reboot, not a boot loop
Only one supervisor crashed, standby is stable — see device.supervisor_failover
Device is permanently unreachable — no cycling, see device.power_failure or device.unreachable
Device stabilizes after 1-2 reboots — single event, see device.unexpected_reboot
Only one supervisor crashed, standby stable — HA event, see device.supervisor_failover
psu_status failed — power causing repeated resets, see device.power_failure
linecard_status failed but chassis is stable — see device.linecard_failure
sysUpTime > 300s consistently — device is stable, not looping
log_patterns: rommon> (repeated boot sequence in console log)
POST: DRAM test FAILED - slot .* bank .*
Image file .* is not valid - bad magic number
boot: failed to load image .* - retrying
System Bootstrap: failed to boot image
boot: file system check failed - unable to mount flash
POST failure: memory test failed at address .*
System image file is .* (not found in directory)

hypotheses.description: The active boot image is corrupted (incomplete upgrade, storage write error) — OS panics during decompression or load and restarts
Software upgrade did not complete successfully — device is trying to boot the new image which is incomplete, incompatible, or incorrectly installed
DRAM DIMM fault preventing successful memory initialization — POST fails on the memory test and the device resets
Filesystem corruption on the boot flash/SSD — the OS cannot find or read the boot image, configuration, or required startup files
A software bug in the current OS version causes a consistent crash during a specific initialization step — watchdog fires and restarts the cycle

render_template: Device {device} is in a boot loop. Uptime: {sys_uptime_seconds}s, reboot count in last hour: {reboot_count_per_hour}. Device availability oscillating. Console access required. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).

title: Device Memory Exhaustion - Control Plane RAM Depletion
description: Device RAM is critically depleted — routing processes are being killed or starved, the routing table may be truncated, and the device is at risk of a crash or partial forwarding failure
root_cause_analysis: Memory exhaustion on a network device is a slow-burn failure that can go undetected until it causes sudden service impact. Unlike CPU overload (which is fast and obvious), memory depletion progresses gradually as routing tables grow, software memory leaks accumulate, or a new feature consumes more memory than anticipated. When the OS runs out of free memory, it begins OOM-killing processes — typically starting with non-critical ones, then escalating to routing processes (bgpd, rpd, ospfd). The result is routing table truncation, BGP session drops due to memory allocation failures for updates, or a full control-plane crash. Critical distinction from device.cpu_overload: CPU utilization may look entirely normal while memory is critically low. Always check memory metrics independently, not as a proxy for device health.

symptoms: memory_utilization_percent persistently >90% or free_memory_mb is critically low
Routing process (bgpd, rpd, ospfd) restarted due to OOM kill
BGP session drops with 'NOMEMORY' or 'insufficient resources' notification
Routing table is smaller than expected — some prefixes missing
Kernel OOM killer log messages or process kill events in syslog
Device may still be reachable but behaving erratically — some routes present, others missing
SNMP hrStorageUsed for RAM rising monotonically over hours/days
negative_indicators: CPU is the primary driver of unresponsiveness — see device.cpu_overload
Device is fully unreachable — memory crash may have escalated, see device.unreachable
Memory utilization is normal — issue is not memory-driven
CPU is the primary driver of unresponsiveness — see device.cpu_overload
Device fully unreachable — OOM may have crashed the device, see device.unreachable
Memory utilization is normal — issue is not memory-driven
sysUpTime very low — OOM caused a crash, now recovering, see device.unexpected_reboot
linecard_status failed — hardware memory issue not system RAM, see device.linecard_failure
psu_status failed — power driving the issue, see device.power_failure
ha_state changed — supervisor HA may have been triggered by OOM, see device.supervisor_failover
log_patterns: kernel: Out of memory: Kill process .* (.*) score .* or sacrifice child
rpd: memory allocation failure - .* bytes requested
BGP: NOTIFICATION sent to .* - error/subcode = 6/0 (Cease/Out of Resources)
OSPF: .* process restarting due to memory pressure
show system memory: free memory .* MB below threshold
hrStorageUsed: .* kB (.* percent of .* kB installed)
Memory: available .* MB - process .* killed by OOM
bgpd: malloc() failed for .* bytes - session dropped
routing-table: truncated at .* prefixes due to memory limit

hypotheses.description: Full Internet BGP table growth (now ~950k+ IPv4 + IPv6 prefixes) has exceeded the device's configured or physical memory capacity for the routing process
A software bug is causing a process (rpd, bgpd, or another daemon) to allocate memory without releasing it — free memory declines monotonically regardless of network load
A recently-enabled feature (e.g., MPLS-TE with large topology, EVPN with large MAC table, NetFlow with high flow rate) is consuming more memory than available
Platform is operating with less physical DRAM than designed for (failed DIMM, DIMM not recognized at boot) — effective capacity is below spec
Disk/flash is full due to accumulated log files or core dumps, causing syslog to report memory-related errors — this is disk, not RAM, but surfaces similarly

render_template: Device {device} memory is critically low: {memory_utilization_percent}% used, {free_memory_mb} MB free. OOM events: {oom_kill_count}. Control plane at risk. Top hypothesis: {top_hypothesis} (score={prior}).
